[PHONOLOGY]

yor:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Bantu noun-class agreement (obligatory on verbs and adjectives, e.g. Zulu, Swahili) has no equivalent in Indo-European; mitigation is to tag nouns with CLASS_TAG and propagate it to modifiers, inserting a pronoun if the target lacks class marking. Kirghiz and Gooniyandi lack grammatical gender entirely, and Grebo's gender system is not sex-based, so gendered-pronoun assumptions misrepresent subjects. (symbol: CLASS_TAG)
- Yoruba (Niger-Congo) uses a pure vigesimal (base-20) numeral system; direct decimal conversion misrepresents quantities and requires base conversion or annotation, since most other families use base-10 or mixed systems. (symbol: [YO]≫num:base20)
[PHONOLOGY]
- Lexical and grammatical tone (e.g. Mandarin's 4-tone system, Yoruba aspect-marking tone, Igbo High/Low) causes roughly 30% ambiguity when translated to atonal Indo-European languages unless explicitly tagged (e.g. #H/#L) and propagated through the DOT so it can be re-inserted as pitch-contour cues in TTS/STT.
- Specific missing phonemes (Japanese /ɸ/, Dravidian retroflex /ɭ/, Turkic /ɲ/, West-African /ɡ͡b/, Afro-Asiatic pharyngeals, Khoisan clicks) have defined substitution targets (e.g. /ɸ/→/f/, /ɭ/→/l/); phonotactic mismatches (Mandarin disallows consonant clusters, Japanese moraic constraints) require epenthesis or gemination when importing loanwords.
[SYNTAX]
- Negation placement in SOV languages varies: NegSOV occurs in Mono (Uto-Aztecan), Yoruba (Niger-Congo), and Sumerian (isolate); SONegV occurs in Mangghuer (Altaic); SO[Neg-V] occurs in Pashto (Indo-European), with further variability in Anufo, Ncàm, and Logoti — systems must adapt to language-specific negation rules rather than assuming a family-level default.

tss:

Rule-based negation generation fails; each irregular form requires a lexical entry, and target languages with unified negative markers will overgenerate regular forms unless all suppletive forms are enumerated; negation particles are mis-generated or omitted in target languages that have a single, regular negative → loss of polarity, potential contradictory statements. (symbol: Single argument indexing)
